questionText: A $12.0\mathrm{V}$ dc battery having no appreciable internal resistance, a $150.0\Omega$ resistor, an $11.0\mathrm{mH}$ inductor, and an open switch are all connected in series. After the switch is closed, what are (a) the time constant for this circuit, (b) the maximum current that flows through it, (c) the current $73.3\mu\mathrm{s}$ after the switch is closed, and (d) the maximum energy stored in the inductor?

parts.label: a
parts.question: the time constant for this circuit

parts.label: b
parts.question: the maximum current that flows through it

parts.label: c
parts.question: the current $73.3\mu\mathrm{s}$ after the switch is closed

parts.label: d
parts.question: the maximum energy stored in the inductor?

questionText: An inductor with an inductance of $2.50\mathrm{H}$ and a resistor with a resistance of $8.00\Omega$ are connected to the terminals of a battery with an emf of $6.00\mathrm{V}$ and negligible internal resistance. Find (a) the initial rate of increase of the current in the circuit, (b) the initial potential difference across the inductor, (c) the current $0.313\mathrm{s}$ after the circuit is closed, and (d) the maximum current.

parts.label: a
parts.question: the initial rate of increase of the current in the circuit

parts.label: b
parts.question: the initial potential difference across the inductor

parts.label: c
parts.question: the current $0.313\mathrm{s}$ after the circuit is closed

parts.label: d
parts.question: the maximum current.

questionText: In the circuit shown in Figure 21.60, the battery and the inductor have no appreciable internal resistance and there is no current in the circuit. After the switch is closed, find the readings of the ammeter $\langle \mathrm{A} \rangle$ and voltmeters $(\mathrm{V}_1$ and $\mathrm{V}_2)$ (a) the instant after the switch is closed; (b) after the switch has been closed for a very long time. (c) Which answers in parts (a) and (b) would change if the inductance were $24.0\mathrm{mH}$ instead?

parts.label: a
parts.question: the instant after the switch is closed

parts.label: b
parts.question: after the switch has been closed for a very long time.

parts.label: c
parts.question: Which answers in parts (a) and (b) would change if the inductance were $24.0\mathrm{mH}$ instead?

questionText: A 35.0 V battery with negligible internal resistance, a 50.0 V resistor, and a 1.25 mH inductor with negligible resistance are all connected in series with an open switch. The switch is suddenly closed. (a) How long after closing the switch will the current through the inductor reach one-half of its maximum value? (b) How long after closing the switch will the energy stored in the inductor reach one-half of its maximum value?

parts.label: a
parts.question: How long after closing the switch will the current through the inductor reach one-half of its maximum value?

parts.label: b
parts.question: How long after closing the switch will the energy stored in the inductor reach one-half of its maximum value?

questionText: A 1.50 mH inductor is connected in series with a dc battery of negligible internal resistance, a 0.750 k$\Omega$ resistor, and an open switch. How long after the switch is closed will it take for (a) the current in the circuit to reach half of its maximum value, (b) the energy stored in the inductor to reach half of its maximum value? (Host: You will have to solve an exponential equation.)

parts.label: a
parts.question: How long after the switch is closed will it take for the current in the circuit to reach half of its maximum value?

parts.label: b
parts.question: How long after the switch is closed will it take for the energy stored in the inductor to reach half of its maximum value?

questionText: A 12.0 $\mu$F capacitor and a 5.25 mH inductor are connected in series with an open switch. The capacitor is initially charged to 6.20 $\mu$C. What is the angular frequency of the charge oscillations in the capacitor after the switch is closed?

A 5.00 $\mu$F capacitor is initially charged to a potential of 16.0 V. It is then connected in series with a 3.75 mH inductor. (a) What is the total energy stored in this circuit? (b) What is the maximum current in the inductor? What is the charge on the capacitor plates at the instant the current in the inductor is maximal?

parts.label: a
parts.question: What is the total energy stored in this circuit?

parts.label: b
parts.question: What is the maximum current in the inductor? What is the charge on the capacitor plates at the instant the current in the inductor is maximal?

questionText: A 15.0 $\mu$F capacitor is charged to 175 $\mu$C and then connected across the ends of a 5.00 mH inductor. (a) Find the maximum current in the inductor. At the instant the current in the inductor is maximal, how much charge is on the capacitor plates? (b) Find the maximum potential across the capacitor. At this instant, what is the current in the inductor? (c) Find the maximum energy stored in the inductor. At this instant, what is the current in the circuit?

parts.label: a
parts.question: Find the maximum current in the inductor. At the instant the current in the inductor is maximal, how much charge is on the capacitor plates?

parts.label: b
parts.question: Find the maximum potential across the capacitor. At this instant, what is the current in the inductor?

parts.label: c
parts.question: Find the maximum energy stored in the inductor. At this instant, what is the current in the circuit?

questionText: An inductor is connected to the terminals of a battery that has an emf of 12.0 V and negligible internal resistance. The current is 4.86 mA at 0.725 ms after the connection is completed. After a long time, the current is 6.45 mA. What are (a) the resistance R of the inductor and (b) the inductance L of the inductor?

parts.label: a
parts.question: What are the resistance R of the inductor?

parts.label: b
parts.question: What are the inductance L of the inductor?

questionText: A rectangular circuit is moved at a constant velocity of 3.0 m/s into, through, and then out of a uniform 1.25 T magnetic field, as shown in Figure 21.61. The magnetic-field region is considerably wider than 50.0 cm. Find the magnitude and direction (clockwise or counterclockwise) of the current induced in the circuit as it is (a) going into the magnetic field, (b) totally within the magnetic field, but still moving, and (c) moving out of the field. (d) Sketch a graph of the current in this circuit as a function of time, including the preceding three cases.
expectedAnswer: a) 0.225 A, clockwise  b) 0  c) 0.225 A, counterclockwise

parts.label: a
parts.question: going into the magnetic field,

parts.label: b
parts.question: totally within the magnetic field, but still moving,

parts.label: c
parts.question: moving out of the field.

parts.label: d
parts.question: Sketch a graph of the current in this circuit as a function of time, including the preceding three cases.

questionText:
DATA A physics student is studying the behavior of an R–L circuit, such as the one shown in Figure 21.30. She measures the potential difference across the inductor $V_L$ as a function of time. According to her lecture notes, $V_L$ should decay with time as $V_L = Ee^{-(t/\tau)}$, where $E$ is the emf of the battery and $\tau = L/R$ is the time constant of the circuit. After closing the switch, she obtains the following data:

| Time (s) | $V_{L}$ (V) |
| --- | --- |
| 0 | 9.0 |
| 10.0 | 4.8 |
| 18.0 | 2.9 |
| 27.0 | 1.7 |
| 35.0 | 1.0 |
| 41.0 | 0.7 |

(a) What is the emf of the battery? (b) Make a semi-log plot of $V_L$ as a function of time. Using a "best fit" to the data, determine the time constant $\tau$ of the R–L circuit.

parts.label: a
parts.question: What is the emf of the battery?

parts.label: b
parts.question: Make a semi-log plot of $V_L$ as a function of time. Using a "best fit" to the data, determine the time constant $\tau$ of the R–L circuit.

questionText: A flexible circular loop 6.50 cm in diameter lies in a magnetic field with magnitude 0.950 T, directed into the plane of the page as shown in Figure 21.62. The loop is pulled at the points indicated by the arrows, forming a loop of zero area in 0.250 s. (a) Find the average induced emf in the circuit. (b) What is the direction of the current in R: from a to b or from b to a? Explain your reasoning.
expectedAnswer: a) 0.0126 V  b) a to b through the resistor

parts.label: a
parts.question: Find the average induced emf in the circuit.

parts.label: b
parts.question: What is the direction of the current in R: from a to b or from b to a? Explain your reasoning.

questionText: An electromagnetic car alarm. Your latest invention is a car alarm that produces sound at a particularly annoying frequency of 3500 Hz. To do this, the car-alarm circuitry must produce an alternating electric current of the same frequency. That's why your design includes an inductor and a capacitor in series. The maximum voltage across the capacitor is to be 12.0 V (the same voltage as the car battery). To produce a sufficiently loud sound, the capacitor must store 0.0160 J of energy. What values of capacitance and inductance should you choose for your car-alarm circuit?

In the circuit shown in Figure 21.63, $S_1$ has been closed for a long enough time so that the current reads a steady 3.50 A. Suddenly, $S_2$ is closed and $S_1$ is opened at the same instant. (a) What is the maximum charge that the capacitor will receive? (b) What is the current in the inductor at this time?

parts.label: a
parts.question: What is the maximum charge that the capacitor will receive?

parts.label: b
parts.question: What is the current in the inductor at this time?

questionText: Consider the circuit in Figure 21.64. (a) Just after the switch is closed, what is the current through each of the resistors? (b) After the switch has been closed a long time, what is the current through each resistor? (c) After $S$ has been closed a long time, it is opened again. Just after it is opened, what is the current through the $20.0\Omega$ resistor?
expectedAnswer: a) 0 A through 30.0-Ω resistor, 3.00 A through 20.0-Ω resistor  b) 2.00 A through 30.0-Ω resistor, 3.00 A through 20.0-Ω resistor  c) 2.00 A

parts.label: a
parts.question: Just after the switch is closed, what is the current through each of the resistors?

parts.label: b
parts.question: After the switch has been closed a long time, what is the current through each resistor?

parts.label: c
parts.question: After $S$ has been closed a long time, it is opened again. Just after it is opened, what is the current through the $20.0\Omega$ resistor?

questionText: How many turns does this typical MRI magnet have?

If a small part of this magnet loses its superconducting properties and the resistance of the magnet wire suddenly rises from 0 to a constant $0.005\Omega$, how much time will it take for the current to decrease to half of its initial value?

If part of the magnet develops resistance and liquid helium boils away, rendering more and more of the magnet nonsuperconducting, how will this quench affect the time for the current to drop to half of its initial value?
choices: A. The time will be shorter because the resistance will increase.
B. The time will be longer because the resistance will increase.
C. The time will be the same.
D. Not enough information is given.

questionText: If all of the magnetic energy stored in this MRI magnet is converted to thermal energy, how much liquid helium will boil off?

In Figure 21.65a, a current pulse increases to a peak and then decreases to zero in the direction shown in the stimulating coil. What will be the direction of the induced current (dashed line) in the brain tissue?
choices: 1 while the current increases in the stimulating coil, 2 while the current decreases
2 while the current increases in the stimulating coil, 1 while the current decreases

questionText: Consider the brain tissue at the level of the dashed line to be a series of concentric circles, each behaving independently of the others. Where will the induced emf be the greatest?
choices: At the center of the dashed line
At the periphery of the dashed line
Nowhere—it will be the same in all concentric circles
At the center while the stimulating current increases, at the periphery while the current decreases

questionText: Which time-dependent graph of the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ in the coil will increase the maximum induced current in the brain tissue? Assume that everything else remains constant.
Which graph best represents the time $t$ dependence of the current $i$ induced in the brain tissue, assuming that this tissue can be modeled as a resistive circuit? (The units of $i$ are arbitrary.)
